So imagine you're trying to learn about the world but you've only ever read books, no movies, no videos, nothing visual. You'd know a lot of words, but you'd miss out on how things actually look and move, right? That's kind of where a lot of AI has been stuck until now. But researchers from Meta's Fair Team, that's their AI research group. And New York University just shared something huge. They've trained a new kind of AI model from scratch using both text and images. And they're tapping into tons of unlabeled video data to make it even smarter. First off, what's an AI model? Think of it like a super smart brain that's been taught patterns from huge amounts of information. Usually, these models learn mostly from text, words and sentences scraped from the internet, like books or articles. But high quality text is getting harder to find, kind of like running out of good books in a library. So these researchers are turning to videos instead, and not just any videos, but unlabeled ones. That means videos without any captions or tags explaining what's happening. It's like giving the AI eyes to watch the world and figure things out on its own, picking up patterns from just seeing actions unfold. Similar to how a kid learns by observing people without needing everything explained, they call this a multimodal model model, which just means an AI that can handle different types of data all at once, like text, images, and even video instead of being limited to one thing. Picture it like upgrading from a basic phone that only does calls to a smartphone that handles texts, photos, videos, and apps seamlessly. Today, let's unpack how AI agents get tested and improved. We're talking about benchmarks, which are basically like report cards for AI. Scoring how well they handle different tasks. First, what's an AI agent? Think of it as a digital helper that doesn't just answer questions, but can plan and do tasks step-by-step, like a personal assistant, organizing your day or solving a problem by breaking it into parts. Now, to make these agents better, developers use benchmarks, sets of challenges to test their skills. But here's the thing. Most benchmarks are like training for a soccer team where the coach only focuses on free kicks, ignoring dribbling, passing, or defense. You end up great at one skill, but clueless about the full game. Step one, developers create these benchmarks as a bunch of tasks, often heavy on things like coding, writing a computer program. Say, because that's easy to measure, like scoring points on a straightforward test, it's straightforward to see if the AI got the code right or wrong. Step two, the AI tries the tasks, and its score reveals strengths and weaknesses. But if those tests only cover a tiny slice of real life, like studies show they match only about 8% of US jobs, ignoring things like teaching, nursing, or sales, the AI might ace coding, but flop in everyday scenarios, just like that soccer player who shines in practice but struggles in a real match. Step three, to fix this, researchers suggest broader tests, like simulating real work situations, maybe planning a lesson as a teacher, or handling a customer query in retail. This way, the AI gets practice in diverse skills, kind of like adding full scrimmages to soccer training. Step four, over time, this creates a feedback loop, bad scores highlight gaps, so developers tweak the AI, run more tests, and improve it. It's like a coach reviewing game footage to build a better team. And that's basically how benchmarks work for AI agents.
